title: Dynamic Plan Revision
concept: Updating the remaining plan steps when a worker fails or returns unexpected data mid-execution.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what is the job of the Executor Worker?

options.text: It makes the very first big plan and never runs anything
It runs a focused loop to finish one sub-task at a time and reports the result back
It only stores memories and never acts

exercises.explain: The Executor Worker takes one sub-task from the plan, works on it in a tight loop, then hands the result back to the planner. It focuses on a single step at a time.

cards.title: The Plan Just Broke
cards.body: You planned a road trip, but a bridge is closed. Do you cancel the whole trip? No, you find a new route. Agents need to do the same when a step fails.

cards.title: What Is Dynamic Plan Revision?
cards.body: It means updating the leftover steps of a plan when a worker fails or returns surprising data. Instead of blindly following the old plan, the agent rewrites the parts that no longer make sense.

cards.title: Like a GPS Rerouting
cards.body: A GPS doesn't give up when you miss a turn. It instantly recalculates a fresh route from where you actually are. A revising agent recalculates its next steps from reality, not the original guess.

cards.title: In Action
cards.body: Plan: search a database, then email the result. The search returns 'no records found.' The agent revises: instead of emailing nothing, it adds a step to try a wider search first.

cards.title: Myth: A Plan Is Set in Stone
cards.body: Some think once a plan is made, the agent must finish every step no matter what. Not true! Good agents change the remaining steps whenever new information arrives.

cards.title: Rigid vs. Revising
cards.body: One agent charges ahead; the other adapts. See the difference when a step fails.

left.text: Worker fails, agent keeps following old steps and produces a broken result.

right.text: Worker fails, agent rewrites the next steps and recovers smoothly.

exercises.prompt: A worker step returns unexpected data mid-execution. What should a plan-and-execute agent do?

options.text: Ignore the surprise and run the rest of the original steps exactly as planned
Update the remaining steps to fit the new information before continuing
Delete all memory and start the app over from scratch
Ask the user to rewrite the code themselves

exercises.explain: Dynamic plan revision means the agent adjusts its leftover steps to match reality. Unexpected data is a signal that the old plan may need updating.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a worker fails, the agent must always abandon the entire task.
exercises.explain: False. A failure is a chance to revise the plan, not quit. The agent can rewrite the next steps and keep going toward the goal.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about handling surprises mid-task.
exercises.sentence: When a worker returns unexpected data, the agent should revise the remaining steps of its plan.
exercises.explain: To revise means to update or rewrite. The agent revises the leftover steps so they match the new situation.

exercises.prompt: Which response was written by an AI planner explaining how it will react to a failed step?

options.text: idk it broke, try again later maybe
The database search failed, so I am revising my plan: I will retry with a broader query, then continue to the email step once I have results.
cool the plan is done bye

exercises.explain: The AI planner clearly names the failure, explains the revised steps, and keeps the goal in view. That structured recovery is the sign of dynamic plan revision.

exercises.prompt: An agent's plan is: (1) fetch a user's order, (2) calculate a refund, (3) send a confirmation email. Step 1 returns 'order not found.' What is the BEST revision?

options.text: Skip to step 3 and email a refund confirmation anyway
Keep calculating a refund for an order that doesn't exist
Revise the plan: add a step to search by a different ID or ask the user to confirm the order number before continuing
Crash and forget the entire task

exercises.explain: When step 1 returns surprising data ('order not found'), the later steps no longer make sense. A smart agent revises the remaining plan—here, verifying the order first—instead of blindly continuing or quitting. That is dynamic plan revision: letting real results reshape the next steps.
